1: Isaiah 24 opens the section modern scholars call the 'Isaiah Apocalypse' (Isaiah 24-27) — the most cosmically scoped section of the entire book. 'Hineh YHWH boqeq ha'arets uvoveleyha, behold the LORD is emptying/draining the earth and twisting its face.' The verb boqeq (to drain/empty, as one drains a bottle) describes the reversal of creation's filling — Genesis 1:11-25 filled the earth with life; now YHWH empties it. 'Twisting its face' (uvoveleyha panim, and distorting her surface) = disfiguring the landscape. The universal scope: not a regional judgment but the whole earth (ha'arets, the land/earth). 'Scattering its inhabitants' (vehefits yosheveiha) = the reversal of the table-of-nations dispersal of Gen 10-11, now through divine judgment rather than linguistic confusion.
2: 'It will be the same for people and priest, for servant and master, for maid and mistress, for buyer and seller, for lender and borrower, for creditor and debtor' — six pairs of social relationships, all erased by universal judgment. The six pairs cover the entire social spectrum: religious hierarchy (priest/people), household hierarchy (servant/master, maid/mistress), economic relationships (buyer/seller, lender/borrower, creditor/debtor). The Jubilee year (Lev 25) temporarily suspended economic hierarchies as a sign of covenant restoration; here the suspension is permanent and judicial, not celebratory. The cosmic judgment levels all social differentiation — death is the great equalizer (Ecclesiastes 9:2: 'the same destiny overtakes all').
3: 'The earth will be completely stripped bare and completely ransacked — for the LORD has spoken' — the double emphatic: 'hibboq tibboq ha'arets vehibboz tibboz, stripped bare it will be stripped bare, plundered it will be plundered.' The absolute infinitive construction (verb + cognate infinitive) in Hebrew creates an unqualified declaration of certainty. 'For YHWH has spoken this word' (ki YHWH diber et-haddavar hazeh) — the divine word guarantees the outcome. The stripping of the earth is both ecological (land depopulated) and economic (all accumulated wealth removed). The earth that accumulated productivity over millennia is stripped to nothing.
4: 'The earth mourns and withers; the world droops and fades; the high and mighty of the earth wither away' — the earth itself mourning: 'avelah navlah ha'arets, the earth mourns and withers/languishes.' The verbs are paired: aval (to mourn, with the physical drooping of grief) and naval (to wither/fade, the botanical dying of unwatered plants). 'The world droops and fades' (umlelah navlah tevel, the world languishes and withers). The 'high and mighty' (merom am ha'arets, the height of the people of the earth = the exalted ones) also wither — the social height that seemed most immune to judgment also withers. The mourning earth is personified as a grieving community whose grief mirrors its physical withering.
5: 'The earth lies polluted beneath its inhabitants, because they have violated the laws, broken the ordinances, and shattered the everlasting covenant' — the theological ground for the ecological judgment: 'ki-charfu tora, for they have transgressed the laws/Torah' (charfu = transgressed/violated, related to cheref = reproach), 'chalfu choq, they have broken the statute' (chalfu = replaced/violated), 'heferu berit olam, they have broken/annulled the everlasting covenant.' The 'everlasting covenant' (berit olam) can refer to the Noahic covenant (Genesis 9:16 — no more destruction by flood), the Abrahamic (Genesis 17:7), or the creation covenant itself. The earth 'polluted' (chanefah, defiled/polluted — the same root as Isaiah 10:6's godless/polluted) by its inhabitants echoes Numbers 35:33 ('bloodshed pollutes the land, and no atonement can be made for the land on which blood has been shed').
6: 'Therefore a curse is consuming the earth, and its inhabitants suffer the punishment they deserve; therefore the earth's population is burned up and only a remnant survives' — the curse-mechanism: 'al-ken alah akhilah erets, therefore a curse devours the earth.' The alah (curse = the covenant oath-curse of Deuteronomy 29:19-27: 'the LORD's wrath and jealousy will burn against him; the curses written in this book will fall on him') consumes the earth. 'Veyesh-mu yoshevei vah, and the inhabitants of it bear their punishment/are desolate.' The remnant: 'venishtear enosh miz'ar, and humans are left, small in number' — the remnant theology applied universally: even in the comprehensive curse, a remnant survives.
7: 'The new wine dries up, the vine withers, and all the joyful sigh with grief' — the suspension of fermentation: 'aval tirosh umlelah gefen, the new wine mourns, the vine withers/languishes.' The new wine (tirosh, the fresh grape-juice, the first product of the harvest = the symbol of firstfruits blessing) is itself mourning — the liquid that was the marker of festive joy now as grief-stricken as the people. The vine (gefen, the cultivated vine of the covenant — Isaiah 5:1-7 the vineyard song; Psalms 80:8-16 Israel as YHWH's vine) withers. 'All the joyful of heart (kol-simche lev) sigh with grief' (ne'enach, groan).
8: 'The joyful beating of tambourines stops; the shouting of the revellers ends; the happy strumming of the harp goes silent' — the three instruments of festive celebration silenced: 'shavat mesos tupim, the joy of tambourines has ceased' (the tof/tambourine was the percussion instrument of the Miriam's celebration, Exodus 15:20; harvest festivals). 'Chadal sheon alozim, the uproar of the jubilant has ended.' 'Shavat mesos kinnor, the joy of the harp has ceased.' The harp (kinnor, David's instrument, 1 Samuel 16:23) silenced. The comprehensive end of music = the end of covenant community life — Psalms 137:2: 'We hung our harps on the poplars... how can we sing the LORD's song in a foreign land?'
9: 'They drink no more wine to the sound of songs; strong drink turns bitter on the tongue' — the corruption of the senses: wine without music and the bitterness of strong drink. 'Beshir lo yishtu-yayin, with song they will not drink wine' — the communal wine-drinking with song (the symposium of festive celebration) ends. 'Yimar shekhar leshoteav, strong drink (shekhar = barley beer/palm wine) will be bitter to those who drink it.' The sensory pleasure of drink is reversed: what was pleasurable becomes bitter. The bitterness may be physical (the taste of anxiety and grief) or metaphorical (joy transformed to mourning).
10: 'The city of chaos lies in ruins; every house is barred so that no one can enter' — the 'city of chaos' (qiryat tohu, the city of formlessness/void). The tohu (formlessness, emptiness) of Genesis 1:2 (tohu vavohu, formless and void) applied to the fallen city: the city has reverted to pre-creation chaos. 'Nishbar kol-bayit mibo, every house is shut up from entry' — the city whose gates and houses functioned as social gathering points (gates for commerce and judgment; houses for hospitality) now barred shut. The social space of the city = community; barred houses = the end of community.
11: 'In the streets there is a cry for wine; all joy has turned to gloom; the gladness of the earth is driven into exile' — the street-cry for wine: 'tse'aqah al-hayayin bachutsot, a cry for wine in the streets' — the desperate longing for what has been removed. 'Arevah kol-simchah, all joy has become dark/gloom' (arav = to grow dark/evening). 'Galah mesos ha'arets, the gladness of the earth is exiled/has gone into exile.' The word galah (to go into exile) is the covenant-judgment word for deportation: Israel's gladness has itself gone into exile, as the people will go into exile.
12: 'Nothing remains in the city but devastation, and the gate is smashed to rubble' — the final state of the fallen city: 'nish'ar ba'ir shoammah, what remains in the city is desolation' (shammah = astonishment/waste). 'Veshafon yoqqe sha'ar, and the gate is battered to ruins' — the gate (sha'ar) as the social, commercial, and judicial center of the ancient city (Ruth 4:1-12: legal transactions at the gate; Proverbs 31:23: 'her husband is respected at the city gate'). When the gate is 'beaten to ruin' (yoqqe, smashed/hammered), the entire social fabric of the city collapses.
13: 'For this is how it will be across the whole earth, among all the peoples — like what remains after the olive harvest, like the few grapes left after the vintage' — the remnant imagery applied globally: 'ki-khoh yihyeh beqerev ha'arets betokh haammim, for thus it will be in the midst of the earth, among the peoples.' The two agricultural gleaning images: 'kenoph zayit, like the gleaning of the olive harvest' (the few olives that remain on the branches after the main harvest) and 'kavolalot im-kallah betsiyr hagefen, like the grape gleaning when the vintage is done' (the straggler grapes left after the pickers). The same remnant imagery as Isaiah 17:6 (two or three olives at the branch top). Universal judgment leaves a tiny remnant.
14: 'The survivors raise their voices and sing; they shout from the west over the LORD's majesty' — the unexpected singing of the remnant: 'hemmah yis'u qolam yaronu, they will lift their voices, they will shout for joy.' The survivors of the universal judgment break into praise 'bege'on YHWH, over the majesty/excellence of YHWH.' The survivors sing 'from the sea' (meyam, from the sea/west = from the Mediterranean coastlands) — the westernmost survivors shout for joy. The theological logic: even in universal judgment, those who survive recognize YHWH's justice and majesty and respond with praise. The praise comes from the extremities of the earth (west in Isaiah 24:14, east in Isaiah 24:15).
15: 'Therefore in the eastern regions, give glory to the LORD; in the coastlands of the sea exalt the name of the LORD, the God of Israel' — the universal praise extended east: 'ba'urim kabbdu YHWH, in the east/lights glorify YHWH.' The ba'urim (in the lights/regions of the dawn = the east) corresponds to the sea/west of Isaiah 24:14 — both extremities of the known world called to glorify YHWH. 'In the coastlands of the sea' (ba'iyei hayam, in the islands/coastlands of the sea) — the Mediterranean island-world (Greece, Cyprus, the Aegean). 'The name of the LORD, the God of Israel' — YHWH specifically as the God of Israel is the object of universal praise, even from regions with no prior covenant relationship.
16: 'From the ends of the earth we hear singing: Glory to the Righteous One! But I say, I am wasting away — I am wasting away! Disaster is upon me! The treacherous keep betraying; the traitors have utterly betrayed!' — the prophet's internal conflict: universal praise from 'the ends of the earth' (mikkanaf ha'arets, from the wing/edge of the earth) — 'Tsevi latsaddiq, glory to the Righteous One!' But the prophet himself says: 'Razziti razziti, I am wasting away, wasting away.' The tension: the prophet hears the eschatological praise but is not yet there; he remains in the present reality of treachery. 'Bogdim bagadu ubeghed begadim, the treacherous keep betraying and with great treachery have the treacherous betrayed' — the fourfold repetition of betrayal (boged) mirrors the universal scope of the judgment he has been describing.
17: 'Terror and the pit and the trap are coming for you, O inhabitants of the earth!' — the triple terror: pachad vaphachat vapach (terror and pit and snare) = a deliberate Hebrew sound-wordplay on three near-homophones. The fear, the pit, and the snare are the three threats that cannot all be evaded: one who flees the fear falls into the pit; one who climbs out of the pit is caught in the snare. The three-stage inescapability describes the comprehensive nature of divine judgment — there is no escape route that leads to safety. Jeremiah 48:43-44 quotes this passage almost verbatim in the Moab oracle.
18: 'Whoever runs from the sound of terror will fall into the pit, and whoever climbs out of the pit will be caught in the trap — for the floodgates of heaven are flung open and the foundations of the earth are shaking' — the Noah flood imagery: 'ki aarubbot mimmarom niftechu, for the windows of heaven are opened' — the same 'windows of heaven' as Genesis 7:11 ('the floodgates of the sky were opened') when Noah's flood began. 'Vayirash'u mosedei aretz, and the foundations of the earth shake' — the cosmic foundations are destabilized (Psalms 18:7: 'the earth trembled and quaked; the foundations of the mountains shook'). The judgment of the Isaiah Apocalypse exceeds even the Noahic flood: the flood was water from above; here both heaven-windows open AND earth-foundations shake.
19: 'The earth is completely shattered; the earth is split wide open; the earth is violently convulsed' — the triple intensification of the earth's collapse: 'ro'a hit-ro'ah ha'arets, the earth is broken, utterly broken' (absolute infinitive emphatic); 'por hitporah ha'arets, the earth is split, utterly split'; 'mot hitmotah ha'arets, the earth is shaken, utterly shaken.' The three verbs with their repeated absolute infinitives create a staccato rhythm of catastrophe. The earth that was YHWH's ordered creation (Gen 1) is now comprehensively disordered. This triple earth-destruction is the cosmic backdrop against which the resurrection hope of 26:19 shines — the same earth that collapses will 'give birth to its dead.'
20: 'The earth reels like a drunk; it sways like a flimsy shelter; its rebellion weighs it down until it collapses and does not rise again' — the earth as a drunk and a shelter. 'Nua tanua ha'arets kashikkor, the earth staggers, staggers like a drunk' — the same drunk-imagery as the corrupt priests of Isaiah 28:7-8, now applied to the entire earth. 'Vehitnodedah kamelunahm, and it sways like a night-hut' (melunah = a watchman's temporary shelter, a flimsy booth). 'Ukhaved aleha pish'ah venaphlah velo-tosif qum, and heavy upon her is her transgression and she falls and will not rise again.' The earth's covenant violation (Isaiah 24:5) is the weight that causes the final fall — the earth falls under its own moral weight, like a drunkard who can no longer stand.
21: 'In that day the LORD will call to account the powers above in the heavens, and the kings of the earth here below' — the cosmic scope of divine accountability: 'yifqod YHWH al-tseva hamerom bammarom ve'al-malkhei ha'adamah al-ha'adamah, YHWH will visit/punish the host of the height in the height, and the kings of the ground on the ground.' The 'host of the height' (tseva hamerom) = the heavenly/spiritual powers behind the nations — the 'princes' of Daniel 10:13, 20-21 (the prince of Persia, the prince of Greece) who are spiritual powers governing nations. Ephesians 6:12 identifies the same reality: 'rulers, authorities, powers of this dark world, spiritual forces of evil in the heavenly realms.' Both the spiritual powers (above) and the human kings (below) face the same divine accountability simultaneously.
22: 'They will be herded like prisoners into a pit, locked up in a dungeon, and after many long days they will be called to account' — the imprisonment of cosmic powers: 've'usfuh asfat al-bor, and they will be gathered, gathered into a pit' (double verb for thoroughness). 'Vesuggeru al-masgger, and they will be shut up in prison' (masgger = the dungeon/lock-up). 'Umerov yamim yippaqedhu, and after many days they will be visited/punished.' The interim imprisonment of the rebellious powers before final judgment echoes 2 Peter 2:4 ('God did not spare angels when they sinned but sent them to Tartarus, putting them in chains of darkness to be held for judgment') and Jude 6 ('the angels who did not keep their proper domain, he has kept in eternal chains under darkness for the judgment').
23: 'Then the moon will be put to shame and the sun confounded, for the LORD of hosts will reign on Mount Zion and in Jerusalem, and his glory will shine before his elders' — the eschatological climax: 'vachefrah halevana uvosheshah hachammah, the moon will be put to shame and the sun will be abashed/confounded.' The two great luminaries created to give light (Genesis 1:14-16) are outshone by YHWH's own glory — the luminaries are made redundant when the divine light appears (Revelation 21:23: 'the city does not need the sun or the moon to shine on it, for the glory of God gives it light'). 'Ki-malakh YHWH tseva'ot behar Tsiyon uviYerushalayim, for YHWH of hosts reigns on Mount Zion and in Jerusalem.' The divine council of 'his elders' (zekenaiv, his elders) echoes Exodus 24:9-11 (Moses, Aaron, Nadab, Abihu, and 70 elders who saw God on Sinai and ate and drank before him).
